tagline: A repository of the birds of the Western Ghats
about_html:
Birds of the Sahyadris

Birds of the Sahyadris is dedicated to the remarkable birdlife of India’s Western Ghats (the Sahyadris), a UNESCO-recognized global biodiversity hotspot. These mountains support roughly 500–600 bird species — about 40–45% of India’s total birdlife — including some of the country’s most distinctive and threatened birds.

About the Project

This guide provides field-tested, plain-language identification for a growing set of Western Ghats species, spanning evergreen forests, shola–grassland mosaics, river valleys, plantations, and hill slopes. Each entry includes practical details on calls, behaviour, habitat, and conservation, paired with local photographs and verified notes. The project expands over time as more species, images, and insights are added.

Western Ghats: Avian Highlights

  • 500–600 species occur across the range seasonally or year-round, from tiny sunbirds and flowerpeckers to large hornbills and raptors.
  • About 29 species are endemic to the Western Ghats — including Malabar Parakeet, Nilgiri Blue Robin, Wayanad Laughingthrush, and Malabar Grey Hornbill.
  • Four hornbill species occur in the Western Ghats, the only Indian region where these many hornbills overlap.
  • Several of India’s most threatened birds — such as the Nilgiri Pipit and Broad-tailed Grassbird — are restricted to high shola–grassland ridges.
  • Over 70 bird families are represented, from bulbuls and babblers to drongos, doves, swifts, raptors, and woodpeckers.
  • Behavioural specialities abound: the Malabar Whistling Thrush can mimic human whistles, and Great Hornbills make powerful, wingbeat-driven whooshes as they traverse forest canopies.

Why the Western Ghats Matter

The Western Ghats are an irreplaceable reservoir of India’s avian diversity. Many of their rarest species face mounting pressure from habitat loss, fragmentation, and climate change. This guide aims to support awareness and conservation by making the birds of the Ghats accessible to beginners and experienced birdwatchers alike.

This field guide is a work in progress. Your feedback, corrections, and photographs from the Sahyadris are always welcome.
